title: Self-Consistent Spectral Quadrature Approach to Many-Body Green Functions
abstract: We develop a self-consistent spectral quadrature (sc-SQ) framework for the calculation of many-body Green functions. The method approximates the Källén--Lehmann spectral measure by Gauss--Christoffel (GC) quadrature, yielding a rational Green function representation with guaranteed spectral positivity that exactly reproduces the first $2N$ spectral moments at pole order $N$. A key component is an SVD-based rank-selection criterion on the Hankel matrix, which identifies the numerically resolvable pole rank $N^*$ from the singular-value gap and acts as a precision-guided diagnostic of correlation complexity. The scheme is made self-consistent by requiring that the spectral function used to evaluate expectation values coincides with the spectral function generated by the quadrature reconstruction. This defines a fixed-point hierarchy that connects systematically to established approximations, including Hartree--Fock and Hubbard-I, and incorporates non-perturbative features such as multi-peak spectral structure. We benchmark the approach for the Anderson impurity model against numerical renormalization group (NRG) results and apply it within dynamical mean-field theory for the Hubbard model on the Bethe lattice. The method captures the three-peak Anderson impurity spectrum and the suppression of quasiparticle weight in the half-filled Hubbard model on the Bethe lattice, including Mott-gap formation on the insulating branch for $N\geqslant 5$, in qualitative agreement with NRG references.
summary: This paper introduces a new self-consistent method for calculating many-body Green functions using spectral quadrature. By using an SVD-based criterion to select the optimal number of poles, the approach captures essential strongly correlated physics, such as the Mott transition and Kondo resonance, more accurately than traditional one-shot methods.
main_problem: The difficulty of calculating many-body Green functions for strongly correlated systems where standard power-series expansions fail to capture non-perturbative phenomena like the Mott transition or Kondo resonance.
main_result: The development of a self-consistent spectral quadrature (sc-SQ) framework that accurately captures the three-peak structure of the Anderson impurity model and the Mott gap formation in the Hubbard model, with results qualitatively agreeing with NRG.
method: A self-consistent framework using Gauss-Christoffel quadrature to approximate the Kallen-Lehmann spectral measure, utilizing an SVD-based rank-selection criterion to identify the numerically resolvable pole rank.
model_or_system: The method is benchmarked using the Anderson Impurity Model (AIM) and the Hubbard model on a Bethe lattice within the Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) framework.
key_observables: Spectral function A(omega), spectral moments, quasiparticle weight (Z), occupancy, and self-energy.
important_parameters: Interaction strength (U), bandwidth (D), hybridization (Gamma), and the quadrature pole order (N).
main_assumptions: The accuracy is limited by the number of resolvable spectral moments and the numerical precision of the input moments; the method relies on the ability to resolve the singular-value gap in the Hankel matrix.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares sc-SQ variants against NRG for the AIM and shows the SVD spectrum; Figure 2 compares one-shot vs. self-consistent approaches in the mixed-valence regime; Figure 3 shows the improvement in spectral resolution as N increases; Figure 4 illustrates the Mott transition and quasiparticle weight.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the sc-SQ framework, establishes mathematical equivalences between quadrature, Padé approximants, and continued fractions, details the SVD-based stabilization and self-consistency algorithm, and concludes with benchmarks against NRG for the Anderson and Hubbard models.
why_interesting: The method provides a non-perturbative, numerically stable way to reconstruct spectral functions and capture complex many-body features like Kondo resonances and Mott transitions without the need for external terminators or diagrammatic expansions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. One-shot SQ for the symmetric Anderson impurity model (𝜀𝑑= −𝑈/2, Γ/𝐷= 0.1, 𝑈/𝐷= 1.0). (a) Spectral function 𝐴(𝜔). Solid blue: NRG reference (digitized from [47]). Dash-dotted red: SQ + SEC [Eqs. (20)–(21)], recovering 𝐴(0) = 1/(𝜋Γ) exactly but with narrow Hubbard satellites. Solid red: SEC + Σ(2) [𝐺0] [Eq. (28)], broadening the satellites to the NRG positions while pre- serving the Friedel sum rule. Triangles: pole positions {𝜀𝑖}, area ∝𝑤𝑖. Dotted: 1/(𝜋Γ) reference. (b) SVD singular value spectrum 𝜎𝑛/𝜎1. The gap above 𝑁∗= 3 exceeds 14 orders of magnitude; dashed: threshold 𝜏= 10−8.

FIG. 2. Comparison of one-shot and self-consistent sc-SQ in the mixed-valence regime (𝑈/𝐷= 1.0, Γ/𝐷= 0.1). (a) Occupancy ⟨𝑛𝜎⟩versus 𝜀𝑑/𝑈. Dashed blue: Hartree seed 𝑛0. Solid red: sc- SQ fixed point (𝑁= 3). Coulomb repulsion suppresses double occupancy well beyond the Hartree estimate; ⟨𝑛𝜎⟩= 1/2 is recovered exactly at 𝜀𝑑/𝑈= −0.5. (b) Spectral function at 𝜀𝑑/𝑈= −0.2. Dotted blue: one-shot result (single dominant peak, see text). Dashed red: sc-SQ fixed point, resolving the three-peak structure. Solid purple: sc-SQ with Luttinger correction 𝜇𝐿(see text), pinning the quasiparticle pole to 𝜔= 0 and recovering 𝐴(0) = 1/(𝜋Γ) exactly. Note: at rank 𝑁= 3 this correction always maps the system to...

FIG. 3. 𝑁-convergence of the DMFT+sc-SQ spectral function for the half-filled Hubbard model on the Bethe lattice across the Mott transition (𝐷= 2𝑡, 𝛽= 100/𝑡). Left: 𝑈/𝐷= 2.0 (metallic, three-peak structure). Middle: 𝑈/𝐷= 2.84 (close to 𝑈𝑐2, strongly correlated metal with sharp central resonance). Right: 𝑈/𝐷= 3.2 (insulating, gapped Hubbard bands). Dotted blue: 𝑁= 3. Dashed orange: 𝑁= 5. Solid green: 𝑁= 7. Dashed black: DMFT+NRG reference (digitized from Bulla et al. [44], Fig. 25(a), rescaled from 𝑊= 4𝑡to our 𝐷= 2𝑡 convention). Shaded grey: non-interacting DOS 𝜌0(𝜔). Metallic-branch curves (𝑈/𝐷= 2.0, 2.84) initialized from exact Lehmann moments of a Caffarel–Krauth ED solver (𝑛bath = 3)....

FIG. 4. DMFT solution of the half-filled Hubbard model on the Bethe lattice with the sc-SQ impurity solver at 𝑁= 7, compared with sc-GW (𝐷= 2𝑡, 𝛽= 100/𝑡) and DMFT+NRG. (a) Local spectral function 𝐴(𝜔) for 𝑈/𝐷= 2 (metallic; blue) and 𝑈/𝐷= 3.2 (insulating; red). Solid: sc-SQ, Lorentzian-broadened (𝜂QP = 0.09 𝐷for the central pole, 𝜂sat = 0.4 𝐷for the Hubbard satellites). Dotted (matching colour): sc-GW (𝜂= 0.025 𝐷).
